title: Verification of the Maximum Temperature Record: A Case Study of Death Valley, 1913

lesson.learn: On July 10, 1913, a temperature of 56.7°C (134°F) was measured at Furnace Creek Ranch within Death Valley National Park, California. This reading remains the highest reliably recorded atmospheric temperature on Earth.
lesson.simpler: A temperature of 56.7°C (134°F) was recorded on July 10, 1913, at Furnace Creek Ranch, Death Valley National Park.
lesson.keyTakeaway: The verification of the maximum temperature record is contingent upon rigorous measurement techniques, including thermometer calibration and systematic observation protocols. Moreover, the atmospheric circulation patterns that contributed to this extreme event, such as the subsidence of the subtropical high-pressure belt, must be comprehensively understood to contextualize the significance of this temperature measurement.
lesson.deeperDive: The extreme heat wave that led to the 134°F reading at Death Valley was influenced by the park's unique geography, including its proximity to the Mojave Desert and the subsidence of the subtropical high-pressure belt. Research suggests that the El Niño-Southern Oscillation (ENSO) event in 1912-1913 also played a significant role in this heat wave. The use of remote sensing technologies, such as weather satellites, has improved our understanding of atmospheric circulation patterns and global climate variability, as exemplified by studies utilizing satellite data to investigate the relationship between tropical Pacific sea surface temperatures and ENSO.
lesson.funFact: Notwithstanding the occurrence of disputed temperature readings, the 134°F record at Death Valley remains one of the most thoroughly verified temperature measurements in recorded history.

people: **Luke Howard's work on cloud classification and Verner Suomi's development of the first weather satellite have advanced our understanding of extreme weather events and remote sensing technologies.**
places: Hadley Centre for Climate Prediction and Research in the UK
years: 1913 (year of the record-breaking temperature in Death Valley)
1958 (year of the launch of the first operational TIROS-1 weather satellite)
1961 (year of the launch of the first operational weather satellite, TIROS-1)
1997 (year of the El Niño-Southern Oscillation event that led to severe heat waves worldwide)
2016 (year of the extreme heat wave in Kuwait)
concepts: Atmospheric circulation patterns (ENSO, subsidence of the subtropical high-pressure belt)
Weather satellites and remote sensing technologies
Heat waves and extreme weather events
Geographical features influencing temperature recordings (altitude, proximity to oceans)
